title: A Year of Us
description: A quiet, reflective moment between Adachi and Shimamura in the gym—a year after they first met. Adachi's head rests on Shimamura's lap as they talk about how much they've changed, and how much they haven't.
content:
It feels like it's been three years since I last saw you, Shimamura.

You're exaggerating. Two years, maybe.

In reality, we'd seen each other yesterday—and again today—but it had been about a year since we were last alone together like this.

...So we're both completely off, huh.

I couldn't tell whether her hair felt warm against my palm or if the heat from my hand was sinking into her instead. Either way, I kept absently stroking her hair as I stared up at the ceiling through the thin ping-pong net.

It was early September, right after school. I don't remember who suggested it, but we drifted to the gym like we always did—on a whim.

Sneaking up to the second floor, trying not to draw attention… The familiar, guilty thrill of it felt almost nostalgic. Two ping-pong tables sat abandoned side by side, and I slid down the wall between them, settling into the narrow gap like it was folding itself around me.

Adachi lay down beside me, her head resting on my outstretched legs.

When I'd asked whether she wanted to be the one offering the lap or the one using it, she'd chosen the latter. She was too shy to look at me, but honestly, having her stare straight up at my face the whole time might've been awkward in a different way.

The gym still had that thick, leftover heat of late summer. Basketballs bounced, sneakers squeaked, and students ran back and forth below us. I kicked off my socks, and every sharp sound made my bare toes twitch. My thoughts were slow, drifting, dissolving into the heavy air.

If I shifted even a little, I brushed up against pockets of warmth dense enough to feel like stepping into a hot bath.

A faint scent of half-melted floor wax lingered, curling lazily through the heat.

This place… hasn't changed at all, I murmured.

The warmth settled over me like gravity. Once you let yourself sink into it, pulling back out took effort. My whole body felt heavy, as if someone had shoved me into an old, dusty mascot costume—the kind you'd see in a forgotten fairy tale. At least the costume could've been cleaned.

Mm. Adachi's head shifted slightly on my thigh. Curled up like a child, she seemed soothed just by being close. She's changed a lot over this past year, but this part of her—this instinct to curl toward me—hasn't changed at all. Whenever we're alone, we always end up like this.

The only real differences are the shift in our relationship—and the color of my hair.

I lifted a strand of my bangs. The soft, sun-washed translucence from before was gone, replaced by a deep, solid black.

A whole year, and my hair had grown back to its original color.

…A year.

It struck me how short that sounded. It's only been a year since I met Adachi.

And yet, when I look back on it, that year feels dense—packed with something weighty and warm, the kind of time that would spill from your hands if you tried to hold it too tightly.

Did anything change for you, Adachi?

Change…? Yeah. I think I've changed a lot.

Her voice was quiet, thoughtful.

And she had. She wasn't the same girl I'd first met. Though her trouble getting close to people—that part hadn't changed.

Back then, she continued, I just thought… staying here with you was fun. That was it.

Mm.

But now… as long as I'm with you, I don't really care where we go. I can finally believe that, honestly. Even when I'm unsure or scared, even when things feel complicated… that feeling doesn't fade. I guess that's the part of me that's changed. Or… that's what I think.

Her voice wobbled, probably from embarrassment. I could feel her lips brush lightly against my thigh as she spoke.

I threaded my fingers through her hair, tracing the curve of her ear.

I see.

No matter where she is—in a closed room, in class, out on the street—Adachi is always the same.

She doesn't long for a wider world. As long as she's beside me, her world is complete.

And I'm not saying that's wrong. No one can take on the whole world, anyway. We don't know what's happening across the planet, or even a few towns over. All we can really do is live within the small slice of the world we can touch.

Being connected to everything was never possible.

So instead of stretching her world halfway, she chose not to stretch it at all… and that, too, is an answer. In her world, Adachi needs only one thing—me.

So… where do you want to go after this?

If I asked her, she'd probably start thinking seriously—almost desperately. That's just who she is: earnest to a fault, always giving everything she has. It still surprises me sometimes. Maybe that's part of why she matters so much—because she isn't just a person to me. She is so many things all at once.

Adachi is affection. A companion. A cage. A barrier. A curiosity. A warmth. A shield. A guide. A routine. A memory.

And she'll become part of my future, too. I just hope she becomes even more than that. I hope her world continues to grow in ways she never expected. And I hope I'll keep being filled by her—and keep living alongside her.
